title: Multi-part thinking

spec.title: Multi-part thinking
spec.notes_for_generator: Break a complex question into parts before asking. Teaches decomposition — that complex questions get better answers when broken into sub-questions first. Hinted: suggest that complex questions are better approached in parts, without prescribing exactly how to break them down.

content:
SUBJECT: AI Gym Day 11: Stop Asking One Big Question

Most business questions are actually five questions wearing a coat. When you ask them all at once, you get an answer that's technically correct and practically useless.

TODAY'S WORKOUT
───────────────
🔍 ANALYSIS | 🔵 STEADY STATE | ⏱ 15–20 min

THE BRIEF:
You're building a business case to justify a new investment — could be hiring, tooling, a new product line, whatever's relevant to your work. Your stakeholders want to understand whether it's worth doing. Before you write a single word of the case, you need to get your thinking straight. The challenge: "Is this investment worth it?" is one of those questions that collapses under its own weight when you lob it at an LLM whole.

YOUR REPS:
1. Before you prompt anything, spend two minutes writing down the distinct sub-questions hidden inside your main question. (Costs? Risks? Alternatives? Timing? Who decides?) Then ask the LLM to help you think through each one separately — starting with whichever feels murkiest. Give the AI enough context about your scenario that it can respond usefully, not generically.

2. Look at what came back. Ask yourself: did the LLM treat all the sub-questions equally, or did it skim some? Pick the one answer that feels thinnest and send a follow-up prompt that pushes specifically on that gap. Don't let it off the hook with a vague answer.

3. Now ask the LLM to pull the threads together — synthesise what you've established across the sub-questions into a single, clear recommendation with the key supporting reasoning. Compare this output to what you'd have got if you'd just asked "is this worth doing?" in Rep 1. The difference is the lesson.

SUCCESS CRITERIA:
Your output is good if:
• Each sub-question produced a meaningfully different answer — not variations on the same point
• The synthesis in Rep 3 reflects the specific reasoning you built across the reps, not generic business-case boilerplate
• A colleague unfamiliar with your scenario could read the final synthesis and understand both the recommendation and the main reasoning behind it

💡 COACHING NOTE:
Complex questions contain hidden assumptions about which parts matter most — and when you ask them whole, the LLM picks those priorities for you. Breaking a question into parts forces you to do the intellectual work first: deciding what actually needs answering. This is called decomposition, and it doesn't just improve your AI outputs — it improves your thinking before you've typed a word. It transfers to any analytical task: writing a strategy memo, preparing for a difficult conversation, structuring a presentation. The person who asks better questions gets better everything.

──────────
Use only material you are allowed to share with your chosen LLM. Anonymise anything sensitive.
Share the programme: https://harrysharman.com/projects/ai-habit/
Found this useful? You can leave a tip: https://ko-fi.com/theaihabit
review_notes: Clarified that AI Habit focuses on chat-based large language models (LLMs), while using AI as public shorthand.
Kept exercises model-agnostic and usable with any text-based LLM or approved work tool.
Reworked research/reasoning language to avoid over-trusting model confidence or hidden chain-of-thought.
Reframed AI Habit as a grounded free 14-day starter block with a paid continuation for deeper 60-90 day habit-building, rather than a validation experiment.

Positioned the paid continuation as The 90-Day AI Habit: a structured applied-LLM programme covering writing/editing, strategy/decision-making, research/assessment, management/delegation, personal productivity, and practical outputs.
Simplified the 90-day commercial route: join individually, bring a small group for a discounted group subscription, or use the workplace launch pack to get an employer to pay.
Added ethical upgrade nudges to Days 3, 7, and 10: endowed progress, career identity, and momentum protection for The 90-Day AI Habit.
Mechanical repair pass: normalised workout badge line after scheduler verification.
